data.title: Broadcom: Isn't An Nvidia Killer, But It's A Growth Superstar Within The AI Ecosystem
data.description:
Follow the Leader has long been a popular children’s game.

Here, “everyone follows and does what the child who is chosen to be the leader does.”

But I wonder if this ever was or is still so in the grown-up world.

Look around.

Consider the 1955 Broadway hit musical Damn Yankees (adapted into a 1958 film and a 1994 Broadway revival). It was about an ordinary guy who, through a deal with the devil, became a baseball superstar determined to help his lowly team conquer the dreaded New York Yankees.

Why wasn’t it called Damn White Sox, or Damn Athletics? Answer: Those teams weren’t special. The Yankees were perennial winners. They dominated baseball. Beating them would be special.

Surf some Reddit groups dedicated to discussion of various businesses. Notice how heavily those who profess love or admiration for Netflix (NFLX) are criticized or downvoted. Yet one quarter's performance or guidance aside, we know NFLX is still a user-engagement powerhouse.

In AI infrastructure, the role of the old New York Yankees is played by Nvidia (NVDA).

On Seeking Alpha, NVDA has more supporters than detractors. But the latter, whatever their numbers, can be quite vigorous in expressions of bearishness. See, for example, here, here, here, here, and here.

The latter link does not retrieve an NVDA write-up. It’s actually for an article on Broadcom (AVGO). Its headline asserts that Broadcom is “Stealing Nvidia’s Lunch Money.”

Others write about how AVGO is working with companies building/offering their own AI chips that supposedly could damage NVDA by competing with its expensive offerings.

Today, I echo a famous 5/1/92 line from Rodney King, who sought to heal Los Angeles riots inspired by criminal-case acquittals of police officers who beat him: “Can we all get along?”

Today, fans of AVGO and NVDA need not heal from violence. All would, however, do well for themselves, and their respective investment portfolios, if they would recognize how each company fits into the overall AI ecosystem and that there’s…

Ample Room For Both AVGO And NVDA

Perhaps devotees of NVDA and AVGO can just get along.

I certainly do not suggest AVGO will overtake, equal, or even come close to NVDA in overall size—or steal its lunch money.

Raw size comparisons don’t interest me.

My interest in AVGO, and the “Buy” rating I assigned on 10/11/24 and will reiterate below, is based on my belief that regardless of what NVDA does or doesn’t do, AVGO can generate more than enough growth to generate excellent returns for those who take positions based on AVGO’s current price.

I feel likewise about NVDA, for which I have a long-pending “Buy” rating. But today, my primary focus is on AVGO.

To give you a sense of what lies ahead for AVGO, consider, as a starting point, its A+ Seeking Alpha Growth Grade and the eye-catching approximately 41% EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) projection.

Based on that, and its approximately 35 P/E (non-GAAP) FWD, we wind up with a very attractive PEG non-GAAP (FWD) of around 0.85.

If we accept the credibility of the 41% growth forecast, the stock’s PEG ratio would make it hard to resist.

And I believe…

AVGO’s Growth Prospects Are Credible

Let’s start with the most obvious aspect of this…

(1) The Current Situation— Sizzling

Preferring the big picture, I don’t usually fuss about numbers discussed on earnings calls. But once in a while, even I feel compelled to call it out.

AVGO reports results with reference to two segments: Semiconductor Solutions and Infrastructure Software.

Here are segment tables showing how the two relate to one another.

Infrastructure’s recently powerful percentage comparisons have been driven by the integration of VMware, acquired in 2023. That’s a nice business—virtualization, which is software that effectively imitates hardware. But going forward, it’s not going to hold a candle to Semiconductor Solutions.

Look at the rightmost table columns. The recent Semiconductor super growth comes from the AI side of that segment.

In the latest quarter, AI semiconductor revenues rose 106% year-to-year in Q1. AVGO is guiding for a 76% Q2 gain here. Custom accelerators grew 140% year-to-year in Q1. And, according to management, this momentum is continuing.

Now, surely you’ve seen big here-today-no-more-tomorrow surges. Speaking of the devil, look no further than AVGO Infrastructure Software. It didn’t maintain its ‘23 and ‘24 growth rates.

Might we see likewise for AI semiconductors? In a word — No!

On the 3/4/26 call, CEO Hock Tan emphasized that…

Our visibility in 2027 has dramatically improved. Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this.

$100 billion in 2027! Look again at ‘25 segment revenue. (Hint: It was just shy of $37 billion.)

All three of AVGO’s big customers, Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Anthropic, are building aggressively and working with AVGO on long-term plans. Also, OpenAI and two other, so far unnamed, large customers came aboard with similar long-range development programs.

And in a sense, these are more akin to strategic partners than mere customers.

They don’t act like consumers taking product off a shelf at Best Buy (BBY) or even an enterprise ordering online from an e-commerce website. Instead, they work closely with AVGO, sharing their plans. collaborating on designs and facilitating AVGO’s ability to properly and timely source inputs.

This gives AVGO uncharacteristically good visibility into its future. And it lends credibility to long-term growth forecasts.

(2) AVGO In The AI Ecosystem — XPUs

If you watch Nvidia to any meaningful degree, you know that its critical AI role depends on much more than the characteristics and quality of its GPUs. It's about CUDA. That’s NVDA’s proprietary software platform and programming environment. Clients use it to put the GPUs to work.

The details are not important in the context of this AVGO report. What’s important to note here is that CUDA adds much to NVDA’s GPUs. CUDA is what propels NVDA beyond being just a chipmaker (even an excellent one). It puts NVDA at the heart of the AI ecosystem.

Similarly, AVGO is about more than just any specific gadget or set of gadgets. It doesn’t offer a CUDA rival. For AVGO, we need to think differently. If we crave a CUDA-like buzzword, I suggest “Enabling.” (This comes from me, not AVGO management.)

Enabling starts with AVGO’s XPUs. The acronym is telling.

PU stands for processing unit.

The G in NVDA’s GPUs stands for Graphics—representing that company’s pre-AI heritage of making chips that handle the needs of super-duper computer graphics (initially for games).

For the X in AVGO’s XPUs, let’s recall back to when we first studied algebra. We learned that X represents the universal unknown, that which we solve for with the algebraic techniques we learned.

In AVGO-world, X is unknown—until AVGO’s customer tells AVGO, in precise detail, what it wants the chip to do. In other words, XPUs are AI chips customized according to customer needs. That’s an important contrast to NVDA’s standardized offerings.

Which is better, custom or standard? According to Tan on the earnings call…

The one size fits all of a general purpose GPU gets you only that far. It can still keep going on because you can still run different workloads…but in a GPU, you're designed for dense matrix multiplication. So you do it with software kernels, but it's not as effective as if you'd hardcode it in silicon and make those XPUs purposely designed to be much more performing….

[Y]ou start to see designs of XPUs become much more customized for particular workloads of particular LLM customers of ours. And the design starts to depart from what is the traditional standard GPU design, which is why, as we always indicated before, XPUs will eventually be more the choice simply because it will allow flexibility in making designs that work with particular workloads, one for training even and one for inference.

It sounds to me like Tan is arguing for the superiority of the custom approach. Other remarks suggested, at least to me, that Tan is suggesting XPU is best for inference, as opposed to training. (While AVGO XPUs and NVDA GPIs can do both, I get a sense that Tan is suggesting GPUs are mainly for training and XPUs are primarily for inference.)

Whether or not Tan would openly endorse that characterization, logic suggests it's at least plausible...

Training—teaching models countless if-then logical connections in order to build their basic knowledge base—sounds like a brute-force kind of task. Doing such things is essentially how NVDA got rich and famous.

Inference is where trained models answer questions and perform user-assigned tasks. This involves less brute force and more finesse, more awareness of the user’s needs, goals, policies, etc. That would seem to lend itself to XPU-like specialization.

GPUs can do it. But here’s a way to visualize GPU versus XPU approaches.

Consider such platforms as Bloomberg, FactSet, or Clarifi.

I’ve never used Bloomberg. But I have used FactSet and Clarifi. Both companies put a lot of resources into supporting users who want to customize each to their own needs. (I used, perhaps, 5% of those platforms’ capabilities.)

I might even say that none of those investment super platforms could stay in business without massive customization capabilities.

I’ve never heard of GPUs having comparable, economically feasible customization capabilities. So when Tan speaks on this topic, I’m an eager listener. And I hear him loud and clear.

Projecting into the distant future, I can envision NVDA improving at customization and AVGO improving at brute force computing. But I think that’s way beyond the horizon of any contemporary investor. That’s why I believe both NVDA and AVGO are important.

In absolute numbers, NVDA may have a much higher ceiling. But when the respective ceilings are compared to the respective market capitalizations of each company’s stock, I see this “battle” as a draw.

(By the way, GOOGL is one of several companies getting a lot of attention for building its own AI chip.) Don’t imagine GOOGL doing that all by itself. It's working hand in hand with AVGO. Actually, GOOGL’s so-called TPU is really one example of an AVGO XPU!)

(3) AVGO In The AI Ecosystem—Beyond the XPUs/GPUs

Whether we’re talking about XPUs or GPUs, AI cannot end there.

Information has to move—and move at blinding speed while losing nothing to the friction of movement.

There are many companies, and a dizzying number of confusing acronyms, that address all this. The acronym I had the most trouble memorizing is SerDes, which is often used together with Retimers.

Imagine 20 freight trains going between the same points at the same time. Rather than have 20 separate parallel tracks, Ser (Serializer) works at the origin to combine it all into one giant train. At the destination, Des (Deserializer) unpacks everything into their respective separate trains.

It’s a long journey, so to make sure no railcars or freight get lost along the way, our mega-train stops at periodic stations (Retimers) to refresh everything.

I can spin your mind with you-don't-want-to-know-how-many other descriptions of other things. But I picked this one as a sample to give you a sense of how important things outside the GPU/XPU are in the overall AI ecosystem. And as noted above, many companies do many things here.

But among them, AVGO is the big gorilla in just about everything that makes data move. Without AVGO’s contributions, GPUs and XPUs would be little more valuable than shiny Lego-like gadgets.

In my 4/13/26 report on Corning (GLW), I discussed scale up (connecting things within a single chip cluster), scale out (connections between clusters within a data center to one another), and scale across (connecting clusters across buildings). AVGO is important in all three levels of connectivity.

Its Tomahawk line of Ethernet switch chips directs traffic between servers and AI processors (GPU/XPU chips). As this product family evolved, each generation took on more traffic capacity and more speed.

Tomahawk 6, introduced in mid-2025, upped the ante via the use of chiplets to boost capacity beyond what any single chip can do. It does that by packaging multiple chips together as a unit. This product is valuable for scaling out. Meanwhile, Tomahawk Ultra aims to scale up connectivity. And completing its offering by serving scale across needs, AVGO has its Jericho line, specifically Jericho 4, designed for longer distances.

Tomahawk and Jericho are designed for hyperscalers and data centers. For enterprise customers, and their cloud computing centers, AVGO offers its Trident chip family. Here, AVGO cuts down on extreme speed in favor of increased versatility and programmability.

In previous write-ups on GLW and Poet Technologies (POET) on 2/6/26, I discussed the growing importance of optics as a much faster transmission medium than copper. This is more a down-the-road than a today thing.

Copper remains king right now. But optics is the future. And AVGO is all over it. The company is already working to establish itself in each aspect of this area (lasers, devices that convert light and electric signals back and forth, encoding, decoding, etc.).

AVGO is also all in on an important area known as CPO (Co-Packaged Optics). I discussed this more fully in my 4/13/26 GLW report. In essence, CPOs bring optical components directly into what had been considered conventional copper-based chips—so copper and optics work hand in hand.

(4) Putting It Together — Enabling

Enabling is what makes AVGO an AI star. Part of that is enabling choice via customized XPUs. And AVGO enables data at all stages of the process to go where it’s needed.

Now, try to imagine AI without NVDA. That may not be possible. GPUs plus CUDA make NVDA that powerful.

Now try to imagine AI without AVGO.

There are lots of competitors competing, sometimes quite well, in various parts of the ecosystem. But AVGO seems on top in terms of breadth of participation and indispensability.

AI without AVGO would likely be needlessly rigid and messy.

That ties AVGO’s growth into that of overall AI, not just where it is today but the future toward which it’s progressing.

And I haven’t even gone into detail on AVGO’s software business. That, too, is likely to gain importance as AI raises the stakes on IT management.

This is why I find AVGO’s projected long-term 41% growth rate credible.

The Numbers

There really isn’t much to say here apart from letting the data speak for itself. AVGO AI is doing well—great, actually—and it has put the overall company on a growth tear.

We see it starting with the quarterly Revenue and EPS tables.

How about that AI-era acceleration!

I’d be tempted to wonder about how long this sort of thing can continue. But management put that to rest during the latest earnings call.

Now, let’s not get too literal here. We're looking at an estimated 68% year-to-year EPS gain this year. Don't climb out onto a window ledge if we see growth winding up, instead at, say, 55% or so. Remember, an estimated 41% 3- to 5-year growth rate gives AVGO a below-1.00 PEG ratio. So there’s plenty of room for an even lesser growth rate to still make us conclude we’re looking at a well-valued stock here.

In that spirit, let’s look at the historical and forward growth rates.

(I compare AVGO to medians among companies in the Semiconductor industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

I know it sounds odd to suggest that the three-year figures are encompassing the Stone Age, or at least the pre-AI era. That’s how fast things are evolving now!

The forward growth rates provide a better sense of where AVGO is going.

Here are the core fundamentals.

What can I say about the returns on assets and equity, other than — Excellent!

Let’s now stray a bit from chronic magnificence.

The company didn’t suddenly spring into existence in its current form when AI hit the scene. It took a lot of work to get here.

This suggests an interesting and acquisition-heavy company history. And that’s exactly what we have here.

I sometimes wonder what AVGO might have become had AI not burst onto the scene.

Might it have turned into a sprawling technology conglomerate? Or did management see all along how the world was likely to develop and build its way there step by step? I don’t know. Are there any aspiring business authors reading this? If so, I think AVGO’s development could be a worthy topic. (At my age, I no longer have the energy to tackle this myself.) Sigh…

Anyway, everything AVGO did cost money. And a lot of it came to AVGO via the debt market. This shows in the first four rows of the Fundamentals table.

We see that the company is more debt reliant than the industry or the SPY universe.

I’m not breaking out into a sweat. AVGO can handle it. Below, we see that in the Financial Highlights table.

Here, you see financial footprints of acquisitions and borrowings.

But healthy operating cash flows can support lots of things. We also see a cash position that kept trending better. We see a long-term debt ratio that’s actually been brought lower in recent years and an interest coverage ratio that’s been rising. And we even see a gently rising dividend as well as share buybacks.

Risk

Following up on the end of the above section, I’ll characterize financial risk here as low.

But I do see other noteworthy risks.

One centers on the possibility of an AI bust. That would hit AVGO hard. I consider bearish rhetoric urging us to learn from the circa-2000 telecom bust to be badly reasoned. See here for more.

I’m more concerned with “event risk.”

Back when I was doing junk bonds, we used that phrase for companies that were doing too well. We feared that too much success would make management too bold and motivate it to pursue new directions, like acquisitions that would eventually cause trouble.

AVGO is doing well enough to wonder about this. Acquisitions that continue to enhance core competence would be fine. But I wouldn’t be thrilled if AVGO were to buy its way into different directions.

One might also consider customer concentration.

Yes, indeed. Just a handful of huge customers dominate AVGO’s revenue base. But that’s the nature of this business.

I suppose some might imagine a romantic scenario in which AVGO also sells XPUs or Tomahawk chips to 500 or so mom-and-pop data centers. Cue the violins, hearts, and flowers.

Splash!

That’s me pouring ice water over such nonsense. We’re past the quarter point of the 21st century and still running fast. More and more, size matters in tech. This is what the AI business is. Scale, or concentration, is what this world is becoming.

So what happens if GOOGL gets into a snit with AVGO and decides to cut ties? Anything can happen. Apple (AAPL) has been doing that with Qualcomm (QCOM). That's been a long process, and it's just been for cell phone modems. Try to imagine how much of a nightmare it would be, not just for AVGO but perhaps more for GOOGL, if the latter had to figure out how to keep selling and using its TPUs if cut off from AVGO's production IP and activities!

There’s always a risk a company can commit something akin to corporate suicide. But if you’re going to lie awake at night worrying about that sort of thing, maybe the best place for your money is short-term Treasury Bills (or under your mattress), but definitely not AI.

And finally, there’s the curse of modern Wall Street—also known as the guidance game. This is where a company reports news that, even while excellent, is not as stupendous as what analysts may have convinced themselves to expect. This is a cloud over all stocks, not just AVGO. Just know it’s there.

What to do About AVGO Stock

Here’s the valuation table.

We touched on this above. The PEG ratio is very attractive and would remain so even if AVGO undershoots the growth target.

I know the EV/Sales ratio looks astronomical. But this ratio is influenced by margin (after all, profit is margin times sales) and sales growth. AVGO is way above benchmark comparisons in both regards.

Here’s the price chart.

The stock had a great run earlier in the past 12 months. But then, it took a well-deserved nap. (No stock can go up every day until the end of time).

But the last earnings report and call amounted to one heck of an alarm clock.

The stock jumped. The recently sleepy 10-day exponential moving average [EMA] jumped above the heretofore lax but now waking up 50-day EMA.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow [CMF] and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trade is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Like the stock price and the 10-day EMA, both of these indicators jumped briskly out of bed.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding, or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” “Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

AVGO may need to catch its breath and get its bearings after having woken up so suddenly. (I’ve been there some mornings. Doesn’t that sometimes happen to you too?) But as investors continue to get comfortable with AVGO’s growth pace, I see its stock as likely outperforming the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by continuing to rate AVGO as a “Buy.”

data.title: Nvidia: The $31 Billion Footnote Nobody's Talking About
data.description:
For the last few years, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) has been on investors' radars for the same reasons: record-breaking quarters. Well, I get it. The numbers are very hard to ignore.

In this piece, I wanted to look at where the stock might go after its next earnings release on May 20, amid the tension in the Middle East. But after looking through the most recent quarterly report and the transcripts, what stood out to me is a vertical hiding in plain sight: it's what connects Nvidia chips once the racks are powered on.

The business is buried within the company’s Data Center segment, and it's grown into a $31 billion annual franchise, yet it's treated like a footnote. That, to me, spells opportunity—and for that reason, and everything mentioned below, my Strong Buy rating for NVDA remains in place.

Where does Nvidia stock stand?

Before anything else, look at the stock.

At the time of publication, NVDA stock trades around ~$208 a share, up a "modest" 12% YTD. I say modestly, as the S&P 500 (SP500) is up barely 5% over the same period.

To me, that's impressive, at least when we think about how growth stocks took a turn for the worse in the first quarter, thanks at least partly to the U.S.-Iran conflict that erupted in late February 2026.

The conflict effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz, sending oil prices soaring above $100 a barrel. Naturally, an environment driven by fear emerged.

And of course, Nvidia, being one of the most talked-about stocks, got caught up in the wave. It dropped alongside the broader S&P 500 (SP500), which fell even harder during the worst weeks of the geopolitical conflict.

Of course, if we look at the chart, NVDA stock made a steady recovery in April 2026, perhaps as a result of the ceasefire announcement. Unfortunately, it appears to be on the verge of collapse as the U.S. continues to impose a naval blockade on Iran.

What do valuation metrics tell us?

Despite the tensions, Nvidia's valuation deserves a closer look because on a forward P/E basis (GAAP), the stock trades at a rather notable discount relative to its semiconductor peers.

I mean, that’s worth pausing.

Look at the peer group: Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) trades at ~77x, Broadcom (AVGO) at ~47x, and Marvell (MRVL)? ~84x.

So, what’s going on here? I think the market is doing what it often does during a period of uncertainty, which is basically painting everything with the same brush.

For example, the political overhang in Iran, as well as margin compression concerns heading into fiscal 2027, have all pushed Nvidia’s multiple to a level that doesn’t align with the fundamentals.

When a company is growing at this pace and is trading below the sector median, that disconnect is where patient investors tend to get rewarded. But of course, what makes me a perma-Nvidia bull goes beyond the current valuations.

What could propel Nvidia higher?

I believe one of the most exciting growth drivers playing out right now is Nvidia’s networking segment, sitting right inside its Data Center segment.

If this networking business were to spin out tomorrow as a standalone company, it would rank among the largest semiconductor businesses on the planet. During the fourth-quarter earnings call, CFO Colette Kress reported that networking pulled in $11 billion in revenue, which was up more than 3.5x from the same period last year.

And if you look at the commentary, it shows that networking revenue hit $10.98 billion in the fourth quarter, up 263% year-over-year, and 34% quarter-over-quarter. Meanwhile, for the full fiscal year, networking topped $31.4 billion, which is up more than 10x compared to fiscal 2020, the year Nvidia closed on the Mellanox acquisition.

As a recap, Nvidia bought Mellanox for ~$6.9 billion about 6 years ago. Today, that $6.9 billion deal has transformed into a division generating $31 billion in annual revenue. That’s roughly 4.5x the purchase price in a single fiscal year’s worth of sales alone. No surprise considering networking is a core technology within the data center, as moving data between processors has become every bit as important as the processing power itself.

I mean, you could have the fastest GPU in the world, but if the data can’t flow between GPUs quickly enough, you’re leaving performance on the table.

During the fourth-quarter call, CEO Jensen Huang confirmed that Nvidia doesn’t ship nodes of computers, but racks of computers, and that NVLink turbocharged the networking business because of the sheer amount of switching each rack requires.

In fact, Huang described Spectrum-X Ethernet as a home run, and that matters more than it might sound, because Spectrum-X is Nvidia’s push into the Ethernet switching market for AI workloads, which is a space historically controlled by Arista Networks (ANET) and Cisco (CSCO).

The point is that by stealing market share (which includes Nvidia-owned InfiniBand), it means the moat is expanding, and I don’t think enough investors are paying attention to that.

Right now, networking makes up about 16% of Data Center revenue, and is growing faster than the computing segment. Again, the full-year network revenue segment rose 142% to over $30 billion, which notably outpaced the 59% growth rate in Data Center compute. That’s unsurprising since networking is starting from a smaller base, but the point is, there’s a compounding effect that’s easy to overlook.

For example, every new GPU rack the company ships generates billions in high-margin networking revenue on top of the chip sale itself, and it's for this reason that networking has grown into Nvidia’s second-largest revenue stream next to compute, and with the upcoming Rubin platform’s NVLink 6 interconnect requiring even more switching per system, the amount of networking hardware sold per rack only goes up.

With that in mind, the company’s revenue guidance for the next quarter (Q1’FY27) is ~$78 billion. If networking holds even its current ratio to Data Center revenue against guidance, this segment alone would be running at a roughly $50 billion annualized rate by the end of the fiscal year. And it's growing at triple-digit rates with strong margins tied to a GPU compute platform without real substitution at scale.

With all of that said, no investment holds up without risks, and the most immediate concern around Nvidia right now has nothing to do with geopolitics.

What could weigh on Nvidia stock?

Nvidia’s gross margins need to be carefully watched, at least right now. The ongoing geopolitical conflict will most likely drag the stock down until it doesn’t, and Nvidia has no control over it. Any selloff driven by fear instead of weakening business fundamentals only serves as a better entry point for investors with long-term horizons or even for those who can wait until the fear subsides.

Personally, when it comes to risks, I tend to focus on the company-specific ones, and as for Nvidia, it’s the gross margin trajectory. This is the percentage of revenue left after the direct costs of making the products, and it’s one of the clearest ways to see how profitably a company sells its goods. For the first time in over 2 years, Nvidia’s margin profile appears to be moving in an uncertain direction.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2027, management guided GAAP gross margin to about 74.9% on a GAAP basis, which is slightly below the mid-70s range investors have gotten used to during the H100 cycle, and what’s driving the minor decline is the Blackwell product mix, specifically the GB200 NVL72 rack scale systems

Those are Nvidia’s most advanced server configurations by far, and because they’re still in early stages of production, they naturally carry higher manufacturing costs, which is typical for a new product ramp, though it doesn’t make the hit to margins any less real.

And there’s also a memory pricing issue brewing in the background, because HBMs – or High Bandwidth Memory – used in AI chips are getting more expensive.

Top Manufacturers of HBMs, like Samsung (SSNLF) & SK Hynix, have limited production capacity due to how strong the demand has become, and for this reason, Nvidia has locked up the majority of supply at higher prices, which ultimately contributed to Nvidia’s near-term margin pressure.

While margin compression risk is definitely present, I think it'll be short-lived. The Blackwell ramp, for example, is front-loaded by nature, which potentially means the heaviest expense is hit early and tapers as production scales.

And perhaps more importantly, this isn’t Nvidia’s first product transition. The H100 cycle, for example, also pressured margins to some extent. It dipped during the early ramp before the margin expanded to all-time highs.

That said, considering how expensive the AI chip market has become, I won’t dismiss this margin risk, and neither should anyone else. For us, investors, the May 20th report will be big. If the 75% margins hold and management reaffirms a mid-70s recovery path in fiscal 2027, the stock could re-rate toward ~30x in relatively short order. Otherwise, we'll see some near-term pressure, at least until margins stabilize.

What’s my verdict on Nvidia?

Hundreds of billions are being spent on AI, and Nvidia is at the center of it. And while some are pouring money into custom silicon and a software ecosystem to reduce their dependence on Nvidia, its orders continue to climb.

The company is definitely in command, but right now, the market is offering it at a discount relative to its semiconductor peers, though I think that window is closing fast. For that reason, and everything mentioned above, my Strong Buy rating for Nvidia remains in place.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 269: $26,900 Allocated, $2,927.54 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
The investment community got another solid week from the market as the S&P 500 closed at 7,165.08, up 0.80% this week. We're in the heart of earnings season as financials set the tone with the offense, then SaaS got taken lower after International Business Machines (IBM) and ServiceNow (NOW) didn't do enough to convince the market that the SaaSpocalypse wasn't an accurate picture of what was actually occurring. We will see where the chips land this week as five of the Magnificent Seven are reporting earnings, and we have a Fed meeting where Fed Chair Powell will give his last address as the presiding Fed Chair. I am excited for this week for two reasons. I have been buying a lot of positions that have indirect exposure to technology, and if these companies crush earnings and move higher, the portfolio will benefit, and if, for some reason, the market sells off, it will give me an extended period to buy assets I want to own more of at better prices. I am getting ready for the most important week of earnings season, and I am excited for the results. The other thing that we need to keep on the radar is if we get a deal with Iran causing the Strait to open and oil prices to fall. If that occurs, it could cause inflation to fall over the next several months, and the Fed could be more inclined to cut rates. If that happens, it will be bullish for several segments of the portfolio, such as REITs, and I am looking to gain more exposure to them before the all-clear sign appears.

After allocating $26,900 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 269 at $37,563.08. The account's profitability decreased (-0.31%) to $10,663.08, which is a return on invested capital of 39.64%. Over the past week, I added to Ares Capital (ARCC). In week 269, the account generated $62.72 in dividend income. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $22.43 (0.76%) to $2,969.13. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $6,485.42 of income since its inception, which is 24.11% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I believe that the situation overseas is going to get better, as there is no incentive for Iran to keep the strait closed. It's costing them too much money, and going to war is the worst possible outcome. I think that if a real deal gets done over the next several weeks, we could see oil go under $70, and by July, we could be looking at the possibility of 2 rate cuts. If I am wrong, I will continue buying assets because the market will likely give us another opportunity, but if I am correct, I will be happy that I continued investing in a market that was volatile over the past month.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio continues to deliver on my goals of mitigating risk and generating recurring income. I have allocated $26,900 to this portfolio, and it is now worth $37,563.08. I am up $10,663.08, or 39.64%, and it is generating $2,969.13 in forward dividend income, which is a 7.9% yield from the portfolio. My yield on cost is still over 11%, which indicates how powerful investing over an extended period of time is while reinvesting the income back into the portfolio. The overall goals of this portfolio were to document how I would build an income-producing portfolio from the ground up. I started with $100. The rules that I have put in place are that no position can exceed 5% and no sector can exceed 20% of the portfolio. I am focused on income and risk mitigation, with capital appreciation being a secondary goal. The results speak for themselves, and if your goals were to mitigate risk while generating income, then this strategy has been doing well. I'm excited for the future, and regardless of what the market does, I will continue to add capital towards this portfolio.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $1,000.27 (33.69%).
  • Equities $643.52 (21.67%).
  • REITs $528.89 (17.81%).
  • CEFs $485.49 (16.35%).
  • BDCs $310.95 (10.47%).

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. In 2025, I collected $2,411.27 in dividend income from 831 dividends. After 17 weeks in 2026, $938.51 of dividend income was produced, which is 38.92% of the total income from 2025. There have been 268 dividends generated in 2026, which is 32.25% of the dividends generated in 2025.

There was $62.72 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $55.21. This is an increase of $8.84 (19.05%) YoY. When I look at the beginning of 2026 compared to 2025, I believe that the income path is going to intensify by a large amount. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

We're headed into the last week in April, and I have generated 97.1

1% of the income from last year. Having generated $185.70 already knowing that many of the monthly paying ETFs and CEFs produce their income at the end of the month is positive for my thesis about not generating less than $200 per month. The way the chart looks, I wouldn't be surprised if I exceeded $225 this month, which would be a big accomplishment. I think the back half of 2026 is going to be big for dividend income. There is a real chance I will exceed $275 in monthly income and potentially break $300 in December. We will see how the year progresses, and I think that by July, I could have my first month of exceeding $250 in 2026 .

There are still 43 positions in the table below that are generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends being reinvested. There's $367.96 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being produced from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it allows me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 39.14% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 21.67% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.86% of the portfolio and generate 78.33% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Just like that, Altria Group (MO) is back over the 4% level without adding a single dollar to it. Sometimes there are incredible value plays and when you take advantage of opportunities, they just continue to compound. The portfolio is much less top-heavy than in previous years, and this has been a goal of mine for quite some time. I am nowhere near my 5% threshold, but I want to keep these positions at a point where I don't feel guilty about adding to them if I feel compelled to. I think there are still many opportunities in the top 20 positions, and I believe that over time, it will even out further.

The top-10 positions didn't change this week, but the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) generated its distribution. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $7,287.18, and they finished the week with a value of $10,701.87. This is an ROI of $3,414.69 (46.86%). There has been $1,619.29 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 22.22% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $753 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.33%. These positions represent 28.49% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.36% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 269 Additions

This week I added to Ares Capital.

Ares Capital Management

  • I have been adding to high-quality BDCs because they are trading under their net asset value (NAV).
  • ARCC closed on Friday at $18.67, which is a -6.37% discount to its NAV of $19.94, which allowed me to purchase a book of assets well under what I would consider their base value without any premium
  • ARCC is also generating $1.94 per share in dividend income, which is a 10.28% yield on the current price
  • I believe that ARCC is the gold standard in BDCs, so whenever I can add at a discount to NAV with a dividend yield that exceeds 10%, I take the opportunity to
  • I believe that ARCC is lower along with many BDCs because they have exposure to smaller software companies, and the narrative on SaaS is causing tremendous fear across these names
  • I will be adding to ARCC in the future as I believe that there is value to be unlocked at these levels

Week 270 Gameplan

This week is reader suggestion week, so please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section. I will be picking a position from the comments or a previously suggested position to add to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Conclusion

When I started this portfolio, there was a lot of pushback regarding the starting amount of $100 and the idea that adding $100 per week wouldn't amount to much. It's not about the amount of capital because everyone has different thresholds they can allocate towards investing. It's about the idea of continuing to invest and adding to those investments on a continuous basis. If you were looking for appreciation and applied this model to an S&P 500 index fund, you would have done very well. I am thrilled with the results, as my goals were to document how I would build an income-producing account from scratch. I am now up 39.64% and have generated $10,663.08 in appreciation while the account is generating $2,969.13 in forward dividend income at a 7.9% yield. Investing is a journey, and this portfolio represents what is possible when you put in the work and stay disciplined. I am expecting that this portfolio will be generating 5 figures of income for me when I retire, with thousands per month being generated.

data.title: New Iranian Proposal Helps Bolster Risk Appetites
data.description:
The breakdown of talks between the US and Iran initially warned of a risk-off session, but a new Iranian proposal appears to have revived the hopes of a resolution. The US dollar (DXY) is trading softer, and equities in Asia-Pacific and Europe rose, while bond yields were under pressure. The front-month crude oil contracts are trading around $2 a barrel higher.

There are two other developments to note. First, with the Justice Department suspending its probe into the Federal Reserve, Warsh is set to be confirmed as the next Fed chair on Wednesday. When Justice Department made the announcement, the implied chances of a Fed cut by the end of Beijing’s shadow trade with Iran, sanctioning shipping vessels and a large Chinese refinery. Lastly, five G10 central banks meet this week, starting the BOJ tomorrow. None are expected to adjust policy, but hawkish holds are anticipated.

Prices

G10

• The euro snapped a three-day slide ahead of the weekend, encouraged by reports that US-Iran talks would continue and that the Justice Department was ending the probe into the Federal Reserve’s renovations. The euro slipped slightly below our $1.1675 target on Thursday before recovering to almost $1.1725 on Friday. The talks were canceled, but news late yesterday that Iran has made another proposal helped the euro extend the pre-weekend bounce, rising to $1.1750 in Europe. Options for 3.4 bln euros expire there tomorrow. The intraday momentum indicators are stretched. Support is seen in the $1.1720-30 area.

• Since mid-March, the dollar has been trading sideways against the yen, mostly between JPY157.50 and JPY160.40. This month, it traded above JPY160 once, and that was only by a few pips. Continued range trading seems to be the most likely near-term scenario. The dollar rose last week for the first time in four weeks, and implied one-month volatility (~7.4%) is at the lower end of where it has been for the last couple of years. These conditions suggest that risk of official intervention is low. The dollar reached almost JPY159.85 at the end of last week and saw JPY159.10 today. It looks poised to recover back toward JPY159.40-50 area in late Asia-Pacific trading today.

• Sterling eased to an eight-session low near $1.3450 on April 23 before recovering ahead of the weekend to slightly above $1.3535 and almost matched last week’s high (a little above $1.3540). It reached almost $1.3560 today. The $1.36 area offers a more formidable cap. Initial support in North America may be around $1.3530.

• The Canadian dollar fell to a five-session low on April 23 and rebounded ahead of the weekend. The greenback rose to about CAD1.3715 after bottoming earlier last week near CAD1.3630. It pulled back ahead of the weekend a little below CAD1.3665 but has taken another step lower today to about CAD1.3610, its lowest level since March 12. The intraday momentum indicators are stretched, cautioning against chasing it in early North American activity.

• The Australian dollar reached four-year highs on April 17 slightly above $0.7220. It pulled back last week and found support in the $0.7110-15 area. It recovered to $0.7155 before the weekend and to $0.7190 today. Expectations are running high that the central bank’s third consecutive rate hike will be delivered next week (May 5). Given the positioning of the momentum indicators, the Aussie may stall again as the $0.7200-20 area is approached.

EM

• The risk-on mood ahead of the weekend helped stem the Mexican peso’s losses after it reached a two-week low on April 23. The domestic news from Mexico was not favorable. President Sheinbaum has seen her support wane in the face of high-profile crimes and the weak economy. The IGAE report on economic activity is seen as a proxy for a monthly GDP report. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey looked for a 0.7% increase in February. Instead, ahead of the weekend, it was announced that it fell by 0.26%. The dollar is trading with a slightly heavier bias today, with support seen near the low recorded on April 23 near MXN17.33.

• The offshore yuan posted its lowest weekly close in three weeks as a consolidative phase unfolded. The US dollar reached a multi-year low on April 14 (~CNH6.8060) and poked above CNH6.84 ahead of the weekend. The greenback fell to almost CNH6.82 today. The PBOC seems to be signaling caution. Coming into today, it had set the dollar’s reference rate higher for six of the past seven sessions. With the greenback, the PBOC lowered the fix today to CNY6.8579 (CNY6.8674 on Friday), its biggest adjustment in nearly three weeks.

• The rupee began today with a five-session losing streak in tow. On April 20, the central bank eased some of the foreign exchange restrictions that it announced earlier this month. The rupee lost almost 1.45% last week, its largest weekly decline since September 2022. It edged slightly higher today. The dollar slipped from INR94.2550, the pre-weekend close, to about INR94.1060 today before settling near INR94.1950.

Other Markets

• Equities in Asia and Europe are mostly firmer. There were a few notable exceptions in the Asia-Pacific region, including Hong Kong, Australia, and Singapore, but Taiwan’s Taiex (TWSE) rose nearly 1.9% and South Korea’s Kospi surged by 2.15%. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up about 0.25% in late morning turnover. If sustained, it would be the largest gain in a little more than a week. US index futures (SPX) are narrowly mixed.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are higher. The 10-year JGB yield rose almost four basis points to 2.45%. European yields are up mostly 2-3 bp. The US 10-year yield (US10Y) is up a little more than one basis point to approach 4.32%. It was below 3.95% before the war began.

• Gold is trading quietly, even if a little softer, within the pre-weekend range. It is about a $30 range on both sides of $4700. Silver is also a little softer and is also within last Friday’s range.

• June WTI is firm near $96.40. It settled about $2 lower before the weekend. Last week’s high was close to $98.40. Last week’s low was slightly below $85.50.

Data

• Ahead of the outcome of the FOMC meeting on Wednesday and the press conference, the US economic agenda is light. The Dallas Fed April manufacturing survey is on tap for today, and more surveys tomorrow (Richmond Fed and Conference Board) and February house prices.

• Mexico reports March goods trade figures. In the first two months of the year, its trade deficit has nearly doubled to about $6.95 bln from $3.55 bln in the Jan-Feb 2025 period. The driver is the 15.2% increase imports, while exports have been solid, rising 12.2%. These are nominal value and could suggest the possibility of a term of trade adjustment.

• The German GfK March consumer confidence survey was in line with the deterioration reported last week by the ZEW and IFO survey (-33.3 vs. -28.1). Pundits talk about China Shock 2.0, and while it is serious, the shock emanating from the US, first from its threat to use force to take Greenland and now the disruptive war in the Middle East, is also powerful. There is little doubt that the ECB, which meets later this week, is on hold. Warnings by Lagarde that significant efforts by governments to cushion energy shock will boost the need for the central bank to hike rates could be cast as a hawkish hold.

• Japan’s leading economic indicator bottomed last April (104.2) and has been trending higher. It reached 113.3 in February, its best level since August 2022. Last week, Japan reported that core inflation fell for the fourth consecutive month in March and was below the 2% target for the second consecutive month. A rate hike tomorrow would be a surprise, and that is a powerful argument against a move. There is around a 72% chance of a hike for the next meeting in the middle of June discounted in the swaps market.

• China reported that Q1 industrial profits rose 15.5% from a year ago, the most in five years. In Q1 '25, they were up less than 1%. To be sure, there is an aggregate estimate, which conceals divergence between sectors. The low return on investment is a symptom of China’s excessive investment. Moreover, it often seems like the competition between regional governments underpins the “ruinous” competition between companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ServiceNow: The Selloff Looks Ugly Enough To Be Interesting (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past few months, I have been trying to stay away from software.

The market has been punishing anything that looks exposed to AI disruption, and I have had little interest in being a contrarian in a sector where sentiment and narratives are not in my favor.

The nearly 20% drop in ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) post Q1 2026 earnings has now pulled my interest back into the software sector.

Counterintuitively, I turned cautiously optimistic on this stock, despite the fact that Q2 operating margin guidance came in at 26.5% versus the Street’s 30.1% expectation and management's cRPO guidance for Q2 implies a slowdown.

There are two reasons. First, the post-earnings selloff feels somewhat capitulatory. In fact, the number of ServiceNow shares exchanged on April 23 is the highest since late 2019 (yes, before the pandemic selloff).

Second, I am not buying the AI disruption fears, even though I continue to remain skeptical of the broader software industry (i.e., the IGV).

Below, I provide the rationale for my rating upgrade.

What Drove the Selloff Post Q1 2026 Results

In my view, the main factor behind the nearly 20% drop (17.75%, to be precise) yesterday was the Armis margin dilution story.

From what I'm reading, the Street was looking at a Q2 operating margin of 30.1%. The actual guidance that management provided came in at just 26.5%, deeply disappointing the market at a time when the AI disruption fears are still ongoing.

The main driver behind the miss was the margin dilution from the Armis acquisition, which was mainly intended to add some revenue exposure in the cybersecurity space. I already discussed my view on this acquisition in my last piece, so here I focus on the new updates.

From what I read in the Q1 earnings call, Armis is expected to hit FY26 subscription gross margin by 25 bps, operating margin by 75 bps, FCF margin by 200 bps, and Q2 operating margin by 125 bps.

That said, there is a double whammy with Armis. As I argued in my last coverage, inorganic growth was one of my main concerns heading into the print. Well, it turns out that Q2 subscription revenue growth, Q2 cRPO growth, and FY26 subscription revenue growth each include about 125 bps of Armis contribution.

In my view, that undermined the fact that the company increased FY26 subscription revenue guidance to $15.735B–$15.775B from the previous range of $15.530B–$15.570B

Let alone the fact that Q1 results came in above the Street's expectations.

Finally, I think it's worth noting another, slightly less obvious factor. Just one day before the Q1 earnings release, this headline came in:

According to the report, it appears that ServiceNow has become a popular short among hedge funds. I generally tend to stay away from companies that are becoming targets for the shorts, especially hedge funds. Now, that said, the short interest is only 2.9%, so this is not GME in 2020/early 2021.

Why I Turned Bullish

I can't remember how many times I said this year that I'm staying away from software. Headlines like the one below should tell you that I'm not a big fan of this pocket in the market:

Let me say this loud and clearly before I continue.

My upgrade on ServiceNow is mainly from a price action and valuation perspective. Yes, there is a fundamental case. However, one could have made the same arguments a few weeks or even months ago, and look where the stock is now.

The first pillar that I want to present is the fact that the company is showing measurable enterprise AI monetization, while the sector is being repriced for AI disruption risk (which, as I argued in my IGV thesis, is completely irrational).

The Q1 print showed the 2026 AI commitments target was raised to $1.5 billion from the earlier $1.0 billion estimate at the end of Q4 last year. Moreover, Now Assist customers are spending over $1 million in ACV, growing over 130% yoy.

You see the disconnect, right?

AI is actually a tailwind for the company. In fact, I believe the increase in AI commitments is largely coming from cross-selling opportunities and not from a narrow AI add-on.

Now Assist continues to see incredible demand, which has had a nice pull effect and driven outperformances across emerging products like AI Control Tower and RaptorDB Pro.- Gina Mastantuono, CFO.

In other words, the company is leveraging its moat: its customer base. That’s why I think, even if someone could vibecode their tools, they could not vibecode their customer base. That is the core reason I think the AI disruption argument is wrong.

Moving on, I actually think the acquisition of Armis (and Veza) may lead to more growth over time. Why?

Well, ServiceNow’s security and risk business crossed $1 billion in ACV in Q3 2025. That gives these two recent acquisitions an already existing commercial base to expand from. So, on top of the inorganic growth that these 2 acquisitions bring, I think ServiceNow may be able to leverage that existing base in its security and risk business to drive organic growth.

Last, but not least, backlog. Q1 cRPO came in at $12.64 billion, representing a 22.6% yoy increase. As shown below, that rate is largely in line with the historical growth in the last few years.

The last 10-Q reveals an interesting data point about the quality of this backlog:

Number of customers with ACV greater than $5 million. We count the total number of customers with annual contract value (“ACV”) greater than $5 million as of the end of the period. We had 630 and 516 customers with ACV greater than $5 million as of March 31, 2026, and 2025, respectively.

Those 630 customers represent a 22% yoy growth vs. a year ago. That said, the guidance for the second quarter implies a 19.5% constant currency growth for cRPO, which is indeed a slowdown in growth despite the 125 bps tailwind from Armis.

I would therefore keep a very close eye on the guidance for Q3 cRPO. If I see another slowdown in growth, I may have to reconsider my rating.

Valuation

After nearly a 65% drop since the highs in January 2025, the stock looks cheap. In fact, the selloff post Q1 earnings feels somewhat capitulatory.

The forward P/E multiple has now compressed to 20x, which is below the tech sector median of 23x. That said, despite the selloff, ServiceNow is still more expensive than its peers, as shown below:

Now, the premium makes sense (to a certain point), given the higher growth rate:

Looking Ahead

I upgrade my rating to a cautious buy, as I believe the selloff was somewhat capitulatory and partly overdone.

On the negative side, I don't like the fact that cRPO is slowing down, with the guidance for Q2 implying 19.5% yoy growth vs. the reported 22.6% yoy growth in Q1. Given that the Q2 cRPO guidance accounted for 125 bps from Armis, the concerns that the company is buying growth (backlog growth in this case) with the recent acquisitions in cybersecurity are somewhat justified.

On margins, I am not excited about the decline in operating margins due to the Armis integration, although my concern is mainly from a growth perspective.

On the positive side, I see clear signs of AI monetization. Looking at Q3, I would keep an eye on whether management’s raised 2026 AI commitment target of roughly $1.5B turns into subscription growth. By subscription, I am mainly looking at large enterprise subscriptions. Therefore, I would keep a close eye on the number of Now Assist customers above $1M in ACV this year.

data.title: American States Water: You Depend On Them, So Own Them
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

If you were to be thrown out into the wild with nothing and no one to assist you, how would you handle that?

This is one reason why I love writing about and researching utilities. For the vast majority of us, including myself, we wouldn't handle being thrown out into the vast wildness of the wilderness with no tools and no support and succeed. When we hear stories about the child who was lost alone on a mountain in Maine and their incredible survival, it fascinates us. For the vast majority of us, we have enjoyed a relatively pampered life when it comes to our basic necessities.

You may not have grown up ultra-wealthy with a golden spoon in your mouth, but you likely still had the ability to walk over to your faucet, turn it on, and water came out. You likely had the ability to flip a switch, and the light came on.

Utilities are often considered basic necessities for everyday American lives. For American businesses, utilities are a must for them to operate efficiently and effectively. Because utilities make up one of the most basic layers of needs within our modern economy and modern civilization, they are essential aspects of our daily lives. They can also play a massively important role within one's investment portfolio. Attractively priced purchases of utilities can provide you with strong returns over the long run and steadily growing dividends. Dividends that may not be the most tantalizing and exciting today but can provide you with exceptional value as they grow into the future.

Part of the Dividend King's Model Portfolio is focused on higher-yield fixed income for today and then growing dividends for tomorrow. Utilities play a wonderful role in being able to benefit both today and tomorrow with their yields, their qualified dividend status, and their growing dividends for the future.

Today, I want to look at a multi-faceted utility focusing both on electricity and water that holds the benchmark as the oldest continuously growing dividend-paying company.

Let's dive in!

The Longest-Reigning Dividend King

American States Water (AWR) is comprised of the following three subsidiaries: Golden State Water Company, Bear Valley Electric Service, and American States Utility Services.

GSWC serves around 265,000 water customer connections throughout Northern, Coastal, and Southern California and made up over three-quarters of 2025 earnings per share (77.4%). BVES serves almost 25,000 electric customer connections in California, with it comprising 7.4% of EPS in 2025. The remaining 18.1% of its 2025 EPS was generated by ASUS, which provides contracted services for water and wastewater systems for 12 military bases under 50-year contracts and one base under a 15-year contract.

Moving forward, the biggest catalyst for AWR is its execution of its 2025 through 2027 General Rate Case. This authorized the company to invest $573.1 million in capital projects over its current rate cycles. AWR is going to use this capital to replace aging water mains and improve fire protection, which will grow its rate base over time (+11.3% from 2021 through 2026). On these investments, the company is authorized to earn a return on equity of 10.06%.

AWR also has the means to finance these investments. The company enjoys an A S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

AWR’s ASUS continues to win U.S. government-awarded contract modifications for new construction projects. In his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, President and CEO Robert Sprowls noted that it was awarded $29.4 million in new capital upgrade construction projects in 2025. These are expected to be completed through 2028. Leveraging its existing relationships, AWR thinks it can effectively compete for new military-based contract awards, too.

That’s why the sole analyst covering the regulated utility projects non-GAAP diluted EPS will rise by 7.3% annually through 2028, off a 2026 base of $3.37. The company’s 2.6% yield is also safe, with the payout ratio likely to be in the mid-50% range for 2026. This is why we believe that AWR’s dividend can grow by around 8% annually (a Chowder number approaching 11), which would lengthen its 71-year dividend growth streak (the longest among all publicly traded companies).

At the current $80.80 share price (April 23rd), the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 21.19. According to data from FAST Graphs, this is well under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 32.3.

Looking ahead, we would argue that a sensible fair value multiple for AWR is 24. That would be more than two standard deviations below the 10-year average P/E ratio. In our view, this compensates for the fact that the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) of 4.3% is much higher than the 10-year average of 2.7%, per Macrotrends.

In recent years, AWR’s earnings yield has achieved relative parity with the 10-year yield. We think this will continue and forecast a low-4% yield in the coming years. That’s how we arrive at our fair value multiple of 24. This signals that shares are trading at a 10% discount to our fair value per share estimate of $89.

If AWR lives up to growth estimates and reverts to fair value, it could log a 13.5% total return through March 2027. By the end of 2031, the regulated utility could put up 8.5% annual total returns.

Notable risks to AWR include a record snowpack drought in the western U.S. and the threat of potential cyber breaches.

Recent data from Climate Central shows that the snow water equivalent for the western U.S. hit a record low for April 1 (when it’s usually around the annual peak) since it began being tracked in 1981. For more context, the amount of water stored in the western U.S. snowpack is 65% below the 1991 through 2020 normal. If AWR doesn’t have enough water supply, that could force it into more expensive water purchases or emergency infrastructure spending. Since that likely won’t be immediately recoverable through rate increases, earnings could at least be temporarily depressed in such a scenario.

Another risk is the ongoing conflict with Iran. Because AWR is a utility with DoD contracts, it’s a high-value target for Iranian-affiliated actors targeting the programmable logic controllers of the U.S. water sector. Recently, several U.S. government agencies issued a warning to U.S. organizations, including those in the water sector, of the urgent and ongoing Iranian-affiliated cybersecurity threat. If a major breach happens, the fallout could include operational interruptions, reputational damage, and significant liabilities.

Conclusion

AWR provides essential services to thousands of Americans every single day. With its exposure to large swaths of the United States, including U.S. military bases. They have the ability to generate strong returns from the capital they invested through the permission of the same regional governments that they serve.

When it comes to your portfolio, having utilities in your portfolio that provide you with strong returns and strong dividend growth is essential. I recommend that as you build your financial fortress, look around you and see what you rely on in your day-to-day life, and then find a way to invest in that to generate returns and dividend income for your portfolio. If you don't have an electric utility, a water utility, or a natural gas utility in your portfolio, you may be missing out on valuable current dividends and dividend growth because of an overlooked opportunity. Don't just buy any utility that you see on the market, though. Make sure that it is attractively priced compared to its historical normal trading price and that it is growing so you can enjoy growing dividends. If you're unsure what to buy, feel free to check out our prior Seeking Alpha Premium content or within Dividend Kings.

Climb above the noise and chaos. Find clarity.

data.title: Lennar: Foundations Remain Solid, But Market Risks, Valuation Flag Caution
data.description:
In the months that followed my previous coverage, Lennar Corporation (LEN) continued to plunge. In a short period, it dropped by 17%, which was a huge disappointment for someone like me who had a buy stance. Somehow, I perfectly understand the market avoidance of this stock considering the challenges surrounding the stock. The housing market has become more volatile in the past year, and recent geopolitical tensions have fueled inflation. Even now, the challenge remains evident. As I looked into its valuation more closely, I had some realizations justifying its weak trend. Technicals adhere to it as reversal tendencies are not present yet.

Lennar: Weakness Continued, But Resilience Was Still There

Macroeconomic volatility in the country persisted despite the efforts of the Fed to bolster economic activity and keep inflation under control. Tariff wars and recent geopolitical tensions did not help, affecting the purchasing of many homebuyers. Even an established name like Lennar Corporation did not shield itself from softer demand and weaker pricing power. We have seen this in its most recent performance.

In 1Q 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $6.6B, down by more than $1.0B, or 13.3% YoY, from $7.6B. This double-digit YoY decrease was much higher than in my previous coverage at only 5.8%. And like in my previous coverage, the continued downward trajectory of revenue hardly indicates strong recovery in the short run. Weaker demand and pricing flexibility in most of its domestic markets drove the huge revenue plunge. Its East, Central, and West segments had lower deliveries and dollar value. This meant that lower home prices did not encourage demand during the quarter. In fact, the median home prices in the US decreased further to $405,300 but remained elevated relative to pre-pandemic levels. Note that in the months of January and February, home sales in the US plunged by 11.3% YoY and by 1.4% YoY, respectively, which easily offset the seasonal strength and recovery in December 2025. On a lighter note, LEN still had a stable performance in South Central, which remained its largest market in housing volume. Home prices decreased a little. However, deliveries rose by more than 300 units, or 6.5% YoY.

Likewise, the operating costs and expenses decreased as well. This shows that LEN was able to optimize its homebuilding in line with the weak market dynamics to maintain efficiency and stabilize costs and expenses. The only problem was that the revenue drop was huge enough to offset their supposedly positive impact. Nonetheless, they helped LEN maintain positive profits. In the end, the net income margin remained positive at 3.8% despite its notable decrease from 6.7% YoY.

Challenges

Inflation remains a challenge for LEN as this continues to squeeze the budgets of potential homebuyers. In fact, it regained its speed to 3.3% last March, which means that prices increased faster than expected. This is much higher than the 2.0% target, which suggests that the Fed still cannot keep it under control. This also shows that the combined impact of tariff wars and the Middle East tension easily pushed prices up. This April, inflation may remain stubborn despite the ceasefire deal between the US and Iran because oil prices remain skyrocketing. In fact, WTI reached its three-year high of $112.84 last April 7, and now, it remains elevated at $96.60. So, the prices of fuel and the affected goods may remain high as well.

Aside from the lower housing budget, LEN may face higher cost pressures because the prices of building materials, especially those it imports, may increase as well. It may also have to raise wages so employees will keep up with the rising prices. In the end, growth and margins may be squeezed further. If not, I believe that its growth prospects may remain limited.

It must also watch out for the potential rate hikes if inflation goes out of hand. The Fed may either halt the rate cut cycle or reverse it. This may discourage borrowers. Also, mortgage rates may rise, so home demand may also be affected. The interest on its borrowings with variable interest rates may increase as well, so it must be more careful.

And for its Q2 2026 performance, I believe that its performance may stay challenged. One aspect to consider is the weak housing market conditions last March. Note that home sales last month fell by 3.6% YoY, while existing home sales hit their nine-month low. This suggests that even seasonal strengths like spring homebuying could still hardly offset the soft market conditions. Because its Q2 2026 is in March, April, and May, the impact may be felt even until today.

Risk Mitigants

Despite the headwinds it faces, I believe that LEN still has some strengths to protect its business. Just to be clear, these are mainly risk mitigants or shields, not growth catalysts.

One aspect to consider is its strategic geographical diversification. LEN has a huge concentration of homebuilding in South Central and East if you look at its home deliveries. These markets include Texas and Florida. These two states have the strongest economies in the US based on GDP. Additionally, they are home to many high-net-worth individuals. For instance, the combined number of HNWIs in Houston and Dallas reached almost 160,000 last 2025. More interestingly, they have enjoyed the highest number of net change in Fortune 500 companies in recent years. More big companies entering them means more job opportunities and higher wages there. So, spending habits in those states may be more resilient.

Its new orders also showed its resilience. You can see that home orders in the East and South Central still increased, although the sales decrease was minimal. This can prove my point about FL and TX. In addition, orders in Central recovered, showing that ample price reductions could work. The problem is that these are still not enough to beat its orders YoY.

Lastly, its liquidity remains robust despite the unfavorable effects on its business operations. Cash and cash equivalents remain large at $2.09B, which can cover more than half of all its debt. In addition, the maturity profile of its borrowings remains decent since over 80% of the total has a maturity of more than one year. To be more specific, 47% has a maturity of at least two years. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of only 0.8x, which means that LEN only needs 0.8 years theoretically to repay the whole amount at once. It means that it is still has low leverage relative. Hence, it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to use the P/E ratio. As you can see, the stock only trades at 13.45x, which can be considered cheap at first glance. However, it trades above its five-year average of 9.37x. It is also the second-highest multiple in those years. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will only be $65.51.

Now, one may say that this doesn't account for its intrinsic value, and that is true. However, it is worth noting that the market reacted adversely to its decreasing earnings. In fact, its TTM earnings have already decreased 13.4%. Meanwhile, its earnings CAGR was -12.7%. This justifies the sharp price downtrend and even shows that the earnings drop was still faster than the price drop.

Technicals

LEN's technicals stay weak as the price decreases with lower highs and lows. You can also see a bull trap from February 2 to 17 when the January 30 low did not break below the December 30 support that led to a temporary rebound. Now, the price trends lower than the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is trending downwards, which shows that recent market sentiments have remained pessimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show renewed buying momentum after the stock became oversold. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line, while the histogram is within positive territory, which means that buying volume outpaces selling volume again. However, you can also see that the histogram and RSI are starting to decrease again as selling pressures emerge again. It is also worth noting that the trend of the two indicators is still consistent with the stock price, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence or reversal tendencies yet.

Conclusion

Lennar Corporation is hammered by weak housing market conditions and macroeconomic volatility. But its strategic geographical diversification and robust liquidity assure us of its sustainability and dividend longevity. Nonetheless, rebound potential is still limited as valuation justifies the weak price trend. Technicals also show that the downtrend may still hold as momentum remains below the past 50 and 200 days, and selling pressures appear again after a brief rebound. I'm downgrading my rating from buy to hold.

data.title: Tomra: Bottom Valuation Opens For An Entry
data.description:
I look for companies that, despite forecasting significant growth, are disliked on the market. This means, in detail, that I have screeners looking for companies that, on a forecasted basis, are set to grow their earnings by either the market average (or sector), while the share price either has significantly declined over time or there's been an event that has caused the share price to dip significantly over a very short time, such as a day or a week.

Tomra Systems ASA (TMRAY) has been on the first list for some time, but it also caused a “ping” from the second list some short days ago, when the stock dipped in conjunction with 1Q. That is not the only event, though you'll see that it has an impact on the share price, to be certain. The secondary event that happened is that the company went ex-dividend. So, all things being equal, at least a 2.15 NOK drop was within the cards due to the company going ex-div. But the thing is that the company dropped far more than this, and this is what seems to be worrying investors and some analysts as well.

So I thought it would be an excellent time to deliver an update on the company.

Tomra is a very tricky company to value properly. This has to do with what the company is, how it has been valued in the past, and what is expected from it in the future. A company that has been excessively premiumized in the past is very difficult to properly estimate, and Tomra has previously traded at P/E multiples of 80x and above. Today it's down below 25x, and 20x and below seems to be in sight. This means that the company would be trading at multiples not seen since about 2017, or roughly 10 years. I've been analyzing the company and its valuation trends for about 2 years and a little more, and I've been both neutral and positive on the company—though my recent ratings have been mostly positive.

Let's see what this current trend and the earnings report bring to the table in terms of valuation estimate.

Tomra—Updating after a significant drop

Tomra, at the time of publishing this piece, is basically a 3-sector play. It works with a collection of recyclables, and it also recycles the recyclables, and it sorts food/provides food sorting technology.

The issue we've had in the past when it comes to this company's trends is that one or the other of these segments has not been great. In this quarter, costs drove the results in the recycling program down significantly, resulting in negative EBITA for the entire segment, despite strong revenues and strong order intake. The food sorting business delivered good, positive results, with EBITA of above €3M, but the heart of the matter is that the company's core, historical operation of collection is the one that still “carries” the business forward in terms of the bottom line.

The fact that Tomra reports strong revenues doesn't necessarily change the outlook on the company for me all that much. Revenues only matter if you can extract profit from them or if you have a plan for extracting profit from them that I can consider to be believable or realistic. A company without a bottom-line profit, meanwhile, can and should be considered a commercial failure by the definition of the term. It consumes more resources than it generates, and it does not generate value for its stakeholders. This very simple approach is something that many investors should pay closer attention to.

Tomra managed over 5% growth at constant currency, despite a seasonally lower volume of throughput. But at the same time, it's important to look at how the company is expanding. It's positive, therefore, that the company has installed 4,000 new machines in Poland alone.
