It's also positive that the company has launched in Portugal, with over 1,000 machines installed or expected to be installed in 1Q. Growth is still very much something that needs to be considered for this business. The company is also launching in Singapore—and this, to me, is a very exciting launch to be sure, especially in conjunction with the new T100 model.

I actually saw this particular model at a trade show, and compared to the T9, it's a very solid upgrade in terms of space, design, and technology, while still using the time-tested features that make the Tomra machine great.

While no single product can “make a company work,” at least very few can. I think this one will be a very impressive upgrade insofar as the company's selection goes.

However, the problem in Tomra isn't its collection segment. It's always recyclables and food sorting. Ever since I started covering the company, it seems there can't be a quarterly report without one of these segments showing a significant drop, despite signs of more of a longer-term slow growth recovery. Recycling is a good example for 1Q26.

Food has actually stabilized now, I believe. For the past few quarters, the swings have been far less than before, and for 1Q26, the segment is showing growth in all markets and types of fruits.

Worth mentioning, the company also has its “Horizon” business segment. I have previously been clear about the fact that I don't think it's a great idea for the company to engage too heavily in this when the earnings and trends are still as wild as we're seeing. The expression of fixing your own house before starting to work on something else comes to mind. The company has been on a buying spree over the past few years, with businesses like C-trace (digital waste), Feedstock (Områ plant, but this one is actually more of a fit for Tomra than I like), and Tomra Reuse (I personally think it's too early for that sort of ambition).

As I said, I'd prefer to focus on the company's actual larger segments rather than a total Horizon segment with less than €11M worth of revenue, which is far less than the current annual CapEx investment, which peaked at nearly €40M in 2024 and which is more than 10x the EBITA generated by the food segment. From this perspective, I understand investors being leery of the company's capital allocation.

The reason for the company's significant drop was its miss in the recycling business. The company had guided for another level of profit, and this was over a 30% miss. It's therefore justified to see some sort of reaction from the company. The lack of commercial activity in NA, driven also by geopolitical tension, was a cause of this as well, and this caused a near-20% drop in the order backlog. It's created worries about whether this entire segment has been misvalued.

Another worry is the drop in gross margins. This is worrying in itself, true, but I view it mostly as a momentary unfavorable mix for the quarter and current period. If it's repeated in the future, I'll re-address it—for now, I think it's not an issue.

The main issue with Tomra is not profit or forecast, but valuation.

Valuation remains the clincher—and it's getting to a “peak,” with profit potential for the savvy investor

As I mentioned, the company's unfavorable legacy of valuation, meaning it was once extremely high, is distorting averages and forecasts and making anything forecastable somewhat uncertain. For over 2 years, I have attempted to find the “right” valuation for the business. In my last article, I gave a PT of about 160 NOK, which is what I'll be working from here.

The 160 NOK level validates a 16-19x P/E for an EPS of about 7-8 NOK, which I believe to be a valid forecast for Tomra in a “working” condition. I am not the only one to believe this, because the current 2027E forecast is above 8 NOK per share, with 8-9 NOK in AEPS in 2028E.

Based upon this, I argue that my estimate is neither out of place nor unrealistic. However, we, of course, need to consider that the company may underperform. It is this that I argue the market is doing. If we assume that the company is going to grow above 30%, or conservatively (discounting), let's say above 20-25%, then it makes sense that a 15-20x P/E might be too low for the company to reflect its true value. Using even just a 19x P/E to 2026E results in 5 NOK, and we get an upside of over 19% annualized.

In previous articles, I've made cases for the company being worth above 160 NOK. Let me approach this from a reverse sort of scenario. The only way that the company here would be worth less than 150-160 NOK is if the company consistently fails to translate its market-leading position in reverse vending into consistent and stable profitability. The recent trading levels, including the drop to less than 100 NOK, reflect an earnings miss and significant uncertainty about the company's near-to-medium-term targets, based on a “hiccup” in recycling demand.

The mistake I believe the market is making is assuming this weakness to be a sign of permanent deterioration. Even if the NA market were to face a structural decline due to higher protectionism, there's ample room in non-NA for growth, which the company is actively pursuing.

It would also require the company to fail in its restructuring program and significant fixed scale effects, meaning consistent unprofitability in recycling with high fixed asset costs and negative operating leverage, as it is called.

It would also require the company to show an inability to replicate high levels of growth in new markets. Recent market launches directly disprove this assertion.

I want to be clear that Tomra has a 70-90% market share in reverse vending. The company is very hard to “assault,” even though some companies like Wincor Nixdorf and Repant are trying. But it's a mountain to climb, because Tomra has established its market leadership over 35+ years.

I say in relation to the above that I consider the likelihood of such structural weakness and underperformance to be low. What I instead do is discount the company's 2-3 year forecast, put the lower P/E at 27-28x P/E, and come up with a PT of 150 NOK after 1Q26.

I do not like the company's foray into Horizons—I don't like many of the businesses they try to work with here, because it feels to me that the company is spread thin and is spreading its bottom line profit thin. It feels to me that the company is “throwing things” at the wall to see what'll stick. In my view, operational focus on legacy areas and underperforming areas should instead be the company's main priority, and the conveying from earnings calls is, to me, (coupled with macroeconomic factors in those segments that the company can do quite literally nothing about) that this is not it.

I therefore increase my discount slightly and reiterate “Buy,” albeit with the clear message that this one may take some time to recover. Catalyst is likely to be a significant AEPS growth.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other industrials similar to Tomra that I consider to be attractive, as well as other interesting investments for both conservative, income-oriented investors and more growth-oriented investors. I also provide more succinct and clear targets and valuations, alongside personal commentary.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Whenever you invest in a company that trades at such a premium, there are risks involved in terms of overpaying and volatile ups and downs. We can see examples of this in the company's historical trading patterns, with significant movements in share price levels in a short time. Beyond those, I believe other risks, including things like evolving regulations, can be offset by the company's positioning in recycling and collecting and where Tomra can “rise beyond” whatever challenges exist and may present themselves.

There may come a time when the company also needs to seriously scale up operations in a way that its current market cap and its current leverage/credit availability don't easily allow for—that's something to keep in mind as well. Though I don't see that currently, I believe the company has done well from a scaling perspective.

Thesis

Tomra is a market-leading, world-leading reverse vending machine and recycling business. At the right price, it becomes a “Must Buy” (below 100 NOK would be my current estimate for that target, and I do maintain that view in 1Q26) with a holding target that goes beyond the usual for a superb business model with proven reach and scalability. It’s one of the most solid future recycling/reverse recycling plays out there.

Tomra remains a play on valuation more than anything else, and because it’s so highly valued, that assumes a very high growth rate. While this is currently expected, I’m more dubious about the exact rate and volume of growth here. This influences how I view the valuation of Tomra.

Anything above 35-40x P/E is, in my view, a no-go for this business, and I want it cheaper. I don't believe sub-25x P/E is realistic, but 25-32x is the most I will pay, and I am still able and willing to pay this here, putting it at around sub-160 NOK. As of this article, I lower my PT to about 150 NOK because of the company's continued foray into Horizon-related investments, which throw off no earnings, while at the same time suffering from its actual operations, such as recycling. This receives low marks from me.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of 150 NOK/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a “Buy” rating as of April 2026. The company fulfills almost all of my fundamental criteria for investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P Global: It's Not About Ratings In Upcoming Earnings
data.description:
S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) investors are growing increasingly frustrated with the stock's underperformance over the past few years, an unfamiliar situation for long-term S&P Global investors who are used to this name being a high-quality compounder.

Many bulls are shrugging off this underperformance, blaming ill-advised AI fears that will evaporate over time, saying that AI will never overcome the network effects of S&P Global's Ratings and Indices businesses.

However, I argue that it's not about those businesses. It's the rest of S&P Global that investors should be worried about.

I am downgrading the stock to 'Hold,' thinking it's far from being as much of a no-brainer as some investors make it out to be.

There Were Warning Signs; Investors That Noticed Early Avoided Underperformance

Over the past five years, S&P Global shares have trailed the market's return by nearly 70%.

In fact, from 2018 on, most S&P Global investors have significantly lagged the market, leading to over 8 years of underperformance.

You know the saying about how you have to do all the right things and then get lucky? Well, I argue that investors who have done the right things knew that something bad was building up for S&P Global.

Simply put, from 2018 onwards, the portion of the S&P Global business that came from the coveted Indices & Ratings segments declined. Even after divesting the Mobility business and some others, and even though they are at all-time highs, we are still below 60% of sales, the level we saw in 2018.

Similarly, the percentage of profits that come from those two businesses has declined as well.

Now, here's where things get interesting.

What Is The Right Multiple For Each Of Those Lines Of Business?

In investing, the easiest way to rate the quality of a certain business is by using earnings multiples. Let's be frank, I don't know a single investor who's investing in S&P Global for Market Intelligence or Commodity Insights, and this should give you an instinct regarding the quality of each business segment.

As for Ratings, we have Moody's Corporation (MCO) as a comparison. Moody's generates more than 70% of its profits from Ratings, and it trades at 27 times earnings. So, let's give a rough assessment that the Ratings business is a ~30x multiple line.

For Indices, we have MSCI Inc. (MSCI). MSCI's Index segment represents nearly 75% of its profits, and it trades at 30 times earnings. I think a fair rough assessment for this business line is a 32x multiple.

Then, there are information services, which are, to be blunt, a commodity line of business at this point. For that, we have FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS), Morningstar, Inc. (MORN), and the other segments of Moody's and MSCI.

Clearly, those are far lower quality businesses, at least that's how the market sees it. I've been saying that for a long time, too. Let's say that anything between 15x and 20x makes sense here.

Alas, we get to a fair multiple for S&P Global, on a weighted basis, at 23.7x earnings.

As we can see, investors who correctly identified this trend had more than enough opportunities to exit while the stock has yet to reflect the dilutive fair multiple. Then came AI fears, and that was the trigger that finally caused the stock to realign.

We'll come back to that fair multiple later.

Preparing For Q1 Earnings Amid Investor Fears

S&P Global is set to report earnings this Tuesday, April 28th, and while Ratings and Indices are the most important lines of business for the company, I expect most of the attention to go towards the other segments, which are the most vulnerable to AI disruption.

In 2025, Market Intelligence and Energy (formerly Commodity Insights) were the slowest-growing segments and continued to have far lower margins. However, these segments exited the year on different notes.

Energy delivered the worst growth level for the entire year in Q4, growing 5.7%, including a minuscule improvement in margins relative to the first half.

Meanwhile, Market Intelligence delivered the highest growth number in Q4, with revenue up 6.6%, though margins here were relatively weak too.

In my mind, good results from Indices and Ratings are all but certain, considering the market performance and issuance activity. As such, all attention will be directed to the rest of the business, which is where investors would want to get some assurance that AI isn't that much of a risk.

Valuation

At $437 per share, S&P Global trades at 22 times forward earnings and 20 times next year's estimates:

This is 6% below our fair multiple assessment of 23.7x, but any further degradation in Market Intelligence or Energy segments could justify a 15x multiple, rather than the 18x we gave, and therefore close the gap.

So, if we assume we're around a fair multiple here, projected annual returns come down to earnings growth:

For 2026, management is guiding for 9%-10% EPS growth, below consensus estimates. Even so, a 9%-10% annual return is 'only decent,' considering it's not that far above the market average, and you're taking on the specific risk of S&P Global, which is no short of uncertainties right now.

Conclusion

I believe investors who are shrugging off S&P Global's recent issues as temporary market panic are making a mistake, as there are clear fundamental drivers for the multiple contraction.

While AI fears seem misplaced when it comes to Ratings and Indices, I think they are very much on point when it comes to the rest of the business, and it's a good portion of the entire group.

Considering there isn't that much of an upside, I am downgrading S&P Global to 'Hold,' finding the risk-reward unattractive.

In upcoming earnings, I would closely monitor the numbers from Market Intelligence and Energy segments to see if AI disruption is already translating into results.

data.title: EFT: No Longer Makes Sense To Stay Invested (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust (EFT), I issued a hold rating due to the possibility of lower payouts as interest rates trended downward. Since then, the fund has seen a double-digit decline in share price, and its total return has materially underperformed against the S&P 500 Index. Since then, the fund has released an updated semi-annual report for the first half of its 2026 period, which prompted me to revisit the fund's performance, earnings, outlook, and dividend coverage. Unfortunately, I believe there may be additional downside risks for investors to consider.

At the time of my last coverage, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 4.7%. Following the deterioration of the debt markets, EFT now trades at a deeper discount to NAV of 10.46%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that EFT now trades at the deeper end of its historical price to NAV range. For reference, EFT has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 4.62% over the last five years. While this may appear as an attractive time to begin accumulating shares, I believe it is actually a direct reflection of the headwinds that the fund continues to face.

EFT now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 7.6%, while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. I anticipate that distributions may continue to be reduced over the next twelve months if the portfolio's net investment income continues to decline. Ultimately, the success of EFT is tied to the outlook of interest rates. A lower rate environment means that the existing holdings will likely produce less interest income. However, a lower interest rate environment can also serve as a catalyst for higher values of its positions. So it can be difficult to estimate which direction the NAV will go over the next twelve months.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, EFT has total net assets of $320.2M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The primary objective is to generate a high current income while also preserving capital over time. In order to achieve this, the fund invests in a range of different floating rate securities. So unlike traditional equities, EFT's success isn't reliant on the earnings growth of its positions. Instead, EFT is reliant on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance over time.

The fund also has a very reasonable management fee of 1.17% of assets, which may be high but acceptable considering the high yield issued to investors. The fund also provides an efficient level of diversity across different industries. For instance, EFT is most heavily allocated to software investments at 11.7% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to machinery and health care providers & services, which accounts for 5.7% and 5.5% of assets, respectively.

However, it is worth mentioning that the specific allocation to software investments can be a risk for the fund in the current market environment. The market continues to punish software companies because of the rising threat of AI. The capabilities and cost-efficiency of different AI tools have become so good that they now threaten the moat that many software companies once had. While this may not necessarily impact their ability to keep up with their debt payments, it could be a source of vulnerability if the downturn in software gets worse.

When assessing EFT's top ten holdings, the fund is so diverse that the weight of the top positions makes up less than 10% of total assets. However, you may notice that EFT is primarily allocated to private entities. This can be great for investors seeking a greater level of diversity away from the debt markets. However, this also means there is less transparency into the health of these entities.

Performance Expectations

Since the time of my last coverage, the fund released a semi-annual report that provides some transparency into the earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.51 per share, while net realized losses landed at $0.22 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the next half of the period, EFT is likely to generate net investment income that sits at the lower end of its historical range. However, this is to be expected in an environment where interest rates will presumably trend lower over the next twelve months. I wouldn't call this a flaw of the fund since it is quite common with funds that hold floating rate securities.

A higher interest rate means that EFT can collect higher interest income from its existing securities. Conversely, a lower interest rate means that the level of interest income collected will also decline. The good news is that these interest rate-sensitive securities tend to share an inverse relationship with the direction of rates. So when interest rates decline, the actual value of its portfolio should increase, which can improve the rate of net realized gains. A good example of this was demonstrated through 2021's rally, which is when interest rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the uncertainty of the pandemic. During 2021, EFT was able to lock in $1.54 per share of net realized gains, which it hasn't been able to achieve since.

So even if net investment income falls in the near-term, there's the potential for these gains to offset the loss of income. For this reason, it can be quite tricky to estimate which direction the NAV will go. Over the last few years, the NAV has struggled to get back to its prior highs, likely due to concerns around defaults and higher interest rates putting pressure on borrowers. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $14.56 per share
  • 2022: $13.24 per share
  • 2023: $12.70 per share
  • 2024: $13.32 per share
  • 2025: $12.81 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $12.56 per share

As part of the portfolio's strategy, EFT utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its returns. Total effective leverage amounts to 35.33% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$175M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during a time when it is more costly to hold debt. A higher interest rate environment means that the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner. Furthermore, the use of leverage can amplify risks if allocated to positions that decline in value.

With all of these structures working together, it is clear that EFT continues to display an inverse relationship with interest rates. Despite the Fed initiating several cuts to interest rates through 2025, the Federal Funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged.

Outlook

With a new Fed chair, Kevin Warsh, the outlook of EFT ultimately depends on which direction interest rates move. It was already stated that the future of interest rate cuts will be 'data-driven,' meaning the Fed may sit on the sidelines and continue observing the markets to see if a rate cut is the right thing to do. Part of me believes this also means there's a good chance that, even with the new Fed chair, interest rates may be left unchanged until the geopolitical issues are sorted out. I doubt the Fed wants to cut interest rates prematurely. However, there are some analysts that believe there will be a handful of cuts over the next twelve months, especially because of Warsh's AI optimism. If interest rates are cut, I believe it will be a catalyst for two reasons:

  1. Relief for existing borrowers and increases the value of positions.
  2. Healthier borrowing environment will spark new investment opportunities for management.

A lower interest rate may cause net investment income to decline in the near-term. However, this can provide some relief for borrowers with thin operating margins, while also increasing the value of these floating rate positions. If less of their earnings is going towards interest expenses, it can translate to better growth. Similarly, an environment of lower interest expenses means there may be an influx of new borrowers in the market. This can provide management with the opportunity to invest in new opportunities to drive earnings higher.

No matter which direction interest rates go, I still have limited expectations around EFT's ability to provide price growth. Investors must remember that the main earnings engine here is the income generation and high rate of distributions, which means the fund isn't structurally built to see large levels of capital appreciation. For instance, EFT's share price has declined by nearly 17.9% over the last ten years, despite market indices racing to all-time highs. When including all distributions paid, the total return jumps up to 78.1% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.069 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.6%. Assuming that EFT keeps distributions unchanged for the next twelve months, we can estimate an annual payout of $0.828 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, EFT may be at risk of not generating enough earnings to support those payouts. It really comes down to whether or not the positions within its portfolio continue to decline in value.

When I previously covered EFT, I stated that the fund is at risk of seeing its payouts decline over time. Since interest rates remain near the higher end of their range, I believe this risk remains. In fact, the distribution has already been reduced twice in 2026, likely driven by reduced net investment income received from its existing positions. While lower rates could eventually spark higher investment activity, I don't think we are there yet.

Despite the cuts, I think it's proactive for management to continue reducing payouts. This helps offset the scenario of failing to generate sufficient income and will reduce the level of NAV deterioration experienced. It is also appropriate to reduce payouts in case the debt markets deteriorate further and face a rising number of defaults. EFT is primarily invested in securities with poor credit ratings, so it can be impacted by defaults in a scenario where interest rates remain elevated. For reference, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 2.03%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 94.57%
  • Not Rated: 3.39%

If EFT were to be impacted by defaults, the earnings would be impacted and further threaten distributions. Therefore, it becomes very challenging to recommend a position in EFT for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income at this time. The time to rotate back into the fund would be when interest rates level off and it is clear that EFT can still grow its NAV with new investments or a more consistent level of net realized gains.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on EFT to a sell at this time. Although the fund trades at a deeper discount to NAV valuation, it is a direct reflection of the weakness the fund is facing at this time. As interest rates continue to trend lower, the fund's earnings will also trend lower. While this can potentially boost the value of its existing positions, there is still uncertainty. Lower earnings also mean that the distributions are likely to trend lower over time, since net investment income will be reduced. Even if rates do trend lower, there's also the risk that these cuts happen slowly, which could cause the debt markets to deteriorate further and cause EFT's NAV to continue its decline. Therefore, it no longer makes sense to stay invested here just for the income.
